caveats: a: OEWS excludes self-employed workers, who may be especially important in small consulting firms.
a: The occupation proxy includes consultants outside marketing consulting and the broader industry includes scientific and technical specialties.
rho: No source was found that measures five-year implementation within the six-digit industry.
rho: The estimate is about operational implementation, not revenue growth, pricing, or demand effects.
e: No public six-digit source was found that separates retainer, repeat-engagement, and one-off revenue or identifies the specified EBITDA band.
e: The NAICS boundary excludes adjacent advertising, PR, direct-mail, and research firms that may have different recurrence.

s5: It does not measure actual control-transfer probability, so the numeric probability is a bounded judgment.

q: This is retention of implemented gross benefit and does not include volume loss or implementation feasibility.
d5: The BLS projection is an employment proxy for a broader industry, not a constant-price, constant-quality output forecast for NAICS 541613.
d5: Employment growth can diverge from client demand if productivity changes materially.

o: The estimate holds service quality constant and does not assume that AI-created categories of work offset displacement.

envelope.interval_scope: research-input sensitivity with dataset anchors (l, n) held fixed

lens.customer_and_revenue_model: Business and organizational clients; recurring retainers, repeat engagements, and ongoing advisory or managed commercial-support fees rather than media spend or a one-time strategy project.
lens.excluded_activities: One-off-only advisory projects, advertising agencies, public-relations agencies, direct-mail advertising, market-research and polling firms, captive internal units, shells, and non-control financing vehicles.
lens.included_activities: US marketing, sales-management, customer-service-management, new-product, pricing, forecasting, and marketing-strategy consultants that supply a repeat or recurring outsourced service to external clients and fall in the lower-middle-market EBITDA band.

a.bridge: O*NET measures tasks for the national Management Analysts occupation, and BLS measures occupations in the broader NAICS 541600 consulting industry, rather than wage-weighted work in NAICS 541613. I map the documented information gathering, data analysis, report preparation, and document-editing tasks to AI substitution or avoided hiring, while excluding client interviews, relationship work, contextual recommendations, and accountable decision-making; this likely overstates exposure for senior relationship-led boutiques and understates it for analysis-heavy retainers.
caveats: OEWS excludes self-employed workers, who may be especially important in small consulting firms.
The occupation proxy includes consultants outside marketing consulting and the broader industry includes scientific and technical specialties.
a.change_evidence: A six-digit NAICS occupational staffing-and-compensation file, time-and-motion studies from marketing-consulting firms, or anonymized task-level timesheets split between retainer and project work.
a.rationale: The task evidence supports substantial exposure in research, synthesis, drafting, reporting, and administrative coordination, but a material share of marketing advice depends on client discovery, market context, persuasion, and senior accountability.

d5.bridge: BLS projects employment in the broader NAICS 541600 Management, Scientific, and Technical Consulting Services to increase from 1,864.7 thousand in 2024 to 2,039.3 thousand in 2034, a 9.4 percent increase. I translate that broader ten-year employment projection into a five-year constant-quality demand proxy, centered below a mechanical half-period extrapolation because marketing consulting faces more direct software substitution than some technical consulting specialties; the proxy may overstate durable demand if clients internalize work.
caveats: The BLS projection is an employment proxy for a broader industry, not a constant-price, constant-quality output forecast for NAICS 541613.
Employment growth can diverge from client demand if productivity changes materially.
d5.change_evidence: A current forecast of US marketing-consulting real output at NAICS 541613, client procurement-spend surveys, and six-digit revenue series deflated with an appropriate service-price index.
d5.rationale: The broad consulting outlook indicates continuing client need for efficiency and specialized advice, while the narrower marketing-consulting basket is vulnerable to digitized research, content, and analytics. The range permits modest real expansion without assuming that all historical consulting demand remains intact.

caveats: No public six-digit source was found that separates retainer, repeat-engagement, and one-off revenue or identifies the specified EBITDA band.
The NAICS boundary excludes adjacent advertising, PR, direct-mail, and research firms that may have different recurrence.
e.change_evidence: Firm-level revenue-recognition data from a marketing-consulting industry survey, QoE reports for marketed firms, or a broker dataset coding retainer concentration and normalized EBITDA.
e.rationale: The classification covers advice on strategy, forecasting, product development, pricing, sales, and customer service. Those activities can be sold on retainer or repeat cadence, but the definition does not require recurrence and a meaningful share is likely discrete project work; the eligibility estimate therefore excludes one-off-only practices.

l.detail: QCEW 2024 private total annual wages at 541613 ($34,007,872,680) x benefits multiplier 1.4294 [ECEC private-industry total comp $46.60/hr / wages $32.60/hr = 1.4294 (March 2026 reference period)] / SUSB 2022 receipts at 541613 ($79,535,987,000). Vintage mismatch: 2024 wages over 2022 receipts, stated. Level: 6-digit. Harmonized to IPS basis ÷1.3407 (median of 65 dual-method codes).

n.detail: margin 0.1565 (margins.json[54]: Damodaran Jan 2026: Business & Consumer Services EBITDA/Sales 15.65%); revenue/employee $282,178 (SUSB 2022 receipts/employment at 541613); class <5: 41,556 firms, avg revenue/firm $395,427 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $61,884 -> out of $1-10M band; class 5-9: 3,444 firms, avg revenue/firm $1,727,383 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $270,335 -> out of $1-10M band; class 10-19: 1,925 firms, avg revenue/firm $3,595,461 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $562,690 -> out of $1-10M band; class 20-99: 1,662 firms, avg revenue/firm $8,903,738 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $1,393,435 -> IN $1-10M band; class 100-499: 274 firms, avg revenue/firm $47,364,223 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $7,412,501 -> IN $1-10M band; class 500+: 186 firms, avg revenue/firm $120,739,011 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $18,895,655 -> out of $1-10M band; n_band = 1,936

caveats: No source directly measures the future operator-required share for this industry.
The estimate holds service quality constant and does not assume that AI-created categories of work offset displacement.
o.change_evidence: Client surveys on replacing external marketing consultants with software or internal teams, plus renewal and churn data for recurring advisory contracts after AI adoption.
o.rationale: Even after software absorbs portions of research and production, clients still need accountable operators to frame commercial problems, obtain internal information, make tradeoffs, persuade stakeholders, and stand behind recommendations. The exposure of routine work prevents treating the entire current service basket as durable.

caveats: Billing mix and pass-through behavior are not publicly reported for this six-digit industry.
This is retention of implemented gross benefit and does not include volume loss or implementation feasibility.
q.change_evidence: Executed client contracts identifying fixed-fee, hourly, and retainer revenue; renewal-price histories; and win-loss records following AI-enabled delivery changes.
q.rationale: Client work is commonly sold by project or hour, with proposals compared on scope, schedule, and cost. That supports partial retention through better delivery economics, but periodic renewal, competitive rebids, and customer demands for lower fees should pass a meaningful portion of gains through to buyers.

caveats: No source was found that measures five-year implementation within the six-digit industry.
The estimate is about operational implementation, not revenue growth, pricing, or demand effects.
rho.change_evidence: A cohort study of marketing-consulting firms showing measured labor-hour reductions after workflow deployment, plus audit results for client-facing AI-assisted deliverables.
rho.rationale: AI can be deployed in internal research, first drafts, analysis, proposal preparation, and routine client reporting, but implementation is constrained by client-data access, source verification, brand and factual risk, bespoke engagements, and the need for senior review before advice reaches a client.

caveats: The available owner-age evidence is for privately held employer businesses generally, not this industry or the EBITDA band.
It does not measure actual control-transfer probability, so the numeric probability is a bounded judgment.
s5.change_evidence: Closed lower-middle-market transaction records tagged to NAICS 541613, broker books showing marketed and closed mandates, and owner-age plus succession-plan survey microdata for this industry.
s5.rationale: Owner-aging evidence creates a succession tailwind, but a qualifying control transfer is narrower than retirement intent: founder dependence, client concentration, and weak recurring revenue can make an advisory practice untransferable. The estimate reflects only the eligible firms under the lens.

principal_driver: Supervised AI can reduce time spent on the research-to-recommendation production chain while recurring client advisory still requires accountable human delivery.
principal_weakness: Public data do not reveal how much NAICS 541613 revenue is recurring and transferable rather than founder-led, one-off strategy work.
